critic_raw:
Overall, this is a carefully designed experiment with several thoughtful controls. Most of the obvious pitfalls (label leakage, test-set reuse, preprocessing leakage, class imbalance handling) are explicitly addressed. However, I identify three substantive methodological concerns worth examining.

**Issue 1: Random session-level splitting violates temporal independence and introduces user-level leakage**

The claim: Stratified random sampling across 180 days of sessions provides valid train/val/test splits.

Why it may be wrong: Sessions from the same user will appear across all three splits. Because the model includes user-level features (user_lifetime_purchases, user_return_rate, account_age) and item embeddings trained jointly, user behavioral patterns in the test set are directly visible during training. A returning user who converts in a test session was likely also present in training sessions — the model learns their behavioral fingerprint. This is not label leakage in the strict sense, but it inflates performance on the test set relative to what would be observed in production, where the model encounters future sessions of known users and entirely new users. The user_return_rate feature is especially problematic: it is computed over the full 6-month window or some historical window, but if it is computed over sessions that span train/test, it encodes future information for some users.

More concretely: user_lifetime_purchases and user_return_rate are aggregate statistics. If these are computed globally over the 180-day window (rather than as of the session's timestamp), a test-time session uses a feature value that was informed by events that occurred after that session — a subtle but real form of future leakage.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Compare performance of the sequence model under random split vs. a strict temporal split (train on days 1–120, val on 121–150, test 151–180). A large gap in favor of random split would confirm user-level or feature-level leakage. (b) Check whether user_return_rate and user_lifetime_purchases are computed as of session start time or globally — if globally, that is a reproducible leakage bug.

**Issue 2: The bidirectional LSTM/transformer encodes future events within the session, which may not reflect production inference conditions**

The claim: A bidirectional sequence model is an appropriate architecture for this task.

Why it may be wrong: A bidirectional LSTM or a full transformer encoder (which attends to all positions) uses future events within the session to predict conversion. In training, the entire session is available (padding to 100 events = full retrospective view). In production, the model may be invoked mid-session (e.g., to serve a retargeting signal before session end). If so, test performance measured on complete sessions does not reflect deployment conditions. Even if scoring always occurs post-session, the use of bidirectionality is appropriate; but the experiment design does not explicitly state that scoring is post-session, and the retargeting framing ('retargeting priority' in Section 5) implies real-time or near-real-time use.

This is not just an engineering concern — it affects the validity of the comparison between the sequence model and the LR baseline. The baseline uses only aggregate features (session_length, etc.) which are by definition end-of-session features. If the sequence model is evaluated post-session, bidirectionality is fine and the comparison is fair. If it is to be used mid-session, the bidirectional model has access to future events it would not have in production, inflating its measured advantage.

What would constitute evidence: Clarify the production inference point. If post-session, no issue. If mid-session, re-run with a causal (unidirectional) architecture evaluated at a fixed prefix length (e.g., first 10 events) and compare to post-session performance. A large drop would confirm the concern.

**Issue 3: The ablation design conflates sequence structure with sequence-derived aggregate statistics**

The claim: The ablation ('sequence model trained without the sequence component') confirms that the sequence itself, not other factors, drives improvements.

Why it may be wrong: The ablation removes the LSTM/transformer layers but retains session_length. However, session_length is itself a summary of the sequence (number of events). A model with sequence access also implicitly knows event ordering, the mix of event types, and interaction patterns that are not summarized in session_length alone. The ablation therefore correctly isolates the contribution of sequence structure, but the baseline LR model already includes session_length — so the ablation tells us the gain from sequence structure over and above duration, but not whether the gain comes from ordering, event-type distribution, or item identity patterns in the sequence. The interpretation ('the sequence, not other factors, drives improvements') is slightly overclaimed given this design.

More importantly: if item ID embeddings are trained jointly end-to-end, they encode conversion signal from the training set. The LR baseline does not have access to item embeddings — only to item_category and item_seller_tier. This creates an information asymmetry: the sequence model benefits not just from sequence structure but from learned item-level representations unavailable to the baseline. An additional baseline of LR + item embedding lookup (without sequence modeling) would isolate whether the gain is from sequence structure or from richer item representation.

What would constitute evidence: Add a third model — LR or MLP on aggregate features plus item embedding averages (bag-of-items) without sequence structure. If this model matches the LSTM/transformer, the gain is from item embeddings, not sequence modeling. If the sequence model still wins, sequence structure is the driver.

**Summary of issues ranked by impact on validity:**
1. Temporal/user-level split leakage (Issue 1) — potentially materially inflates reported test performance and is the most structurally important flaw.
2. Bidirectionality vs. production inference point (Issue 2) — matters only if scoring is mid-session, but the retargeting framing makes this worth verifying.
3. Ablation does not isolate item embeddings from sequence structure (Issue 3) — affects interpretability of results but not the validity of the primary comparison per se.

The methodology is otherwise sound: label window exclusion is correct, preprocessing is properly sequenced, test set is touched once, primary metric (log loss) is well-motivated for the downstream task, and the baseline receives equivalent tuning.
all_issues_raised: Random session-level splitting across 180 days allows the same user to appear in train and test, and user aggregate features (user_return_rate, user_lifetime_purchases) may be computed globally rather than as of session time, introducing user-level leakage and potential future-feature contamination.

A bidirectional LSTM or full transformer encoder uses future within-session events at inference time, which may not reflect production conditions if the model is invoked mid-session for retargeting, invalidating the comparison with the post-session LR baseline.

The ablation isolates sequence structure but does not separate the contribution of jointly trained item ID embeddings from the contribution of sequential ordering, so the claim that 'the sequence drives improvements' may conflate richer item representations with sequence modeling.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random session-level splitting across 180 days allows the same user to appear in train and test, and user aggregate features (user_return_rate, user_lifetime_purchases) may be computed globally rather than as of session time, introducing user-level leakage and potential future-feature contamination.

A bidirectional LSTM or full transformer encoder uses future within-session events at inference time, which may not reflect production conditions if the model is invoked mid-session for retargeting, invalidating the comparison with the post-session LR baseline.

The ablation isolates sequence structure but does not separate the contribution of jointly trained item ID embeddings from the contribution of sequential ordering, so the claim that 'the sequence drives improvements' may conflate richer item representations with sequence modeling.
